architecture_summary: MLP-Mixer is an architecture based exclusively on multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs), with token-mixing MLPs applied across patches and channel-mixing MLPs applied independently within each patch. It uses per-patch linear embeddings, repeated Mixer layers with skip-connections and layer normalization, and a global average pooling classifier head, without convolutions or self-attention.

novel_contributions: An architecture based exclusively on multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) for vision, without convolutions or self-attention.
A separation of token-mixing and channel-mixing MLP layers to mix information across spatial locations and feature channels.
Competitive image classification performance with pre-training and inference cost comparable to state-of-the-art models.
Evidence that Mixer can be competitive on large-scale pre-training and that its performance improves with larger datasets.

key_claims.claim: MLP-Mixer is an architecture based exclusively on MLPs, and does not use convolutions or self-attention.

When trained on large datasets, or with modern regularization schemes, MLP-Mixer attains competitive scores on image classification benchmarks with pre-training and inference cost comparable to state-of-the-art models.
Mixer-H/14 achieves 87.94% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet and is 0.5% better than BiT-ResNet152x4 and only 0.5% lower than ViT-H/14.
Mixer-B/16 attains 76.44% top-1 accuracy when trained from scratch on ImageNet, but tends to overfit and is behind state-of-the-art CNNs/hybrids in the from-scratch setting.
key_claims.evidence_section: 3.2 The role of the model scale

key_claims.claim: Mixer benefits strongly from larger pre-training datasets, and the performance gap between Mixer-L/16 and ViT-L/16 shrinks with data scale.
key_claims.evidence_section: 3.3 The role of the pre-training dataset size

key_claims.claim: Mixer is invariant to the order of patches and pixels within patches, and its performance drops much less than ResNet50x1 under global pixel shuffling.
The learned token-mixing units show global communication and some features operate on the entire image, while deeper layers appear to have no clearly identifiable structure.
